title: The Solar System
teaser: Explore our cosmic neighborhood — from the blazing Sun to distant icy planets, moons, and the mysteries beyond.

summary.intro: The Solar System is the name for the Sun and everything that travels around it — eight planets, dozens of moons, countless asteroids, and icy comets journeying through billions of miles of space. It formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a giant cloud of gas and dust.

sections.title: Our Star: The Sun
sections.body: At the very center of our Solar System sits the Sun — a massive, glowing ball of gas so large that about one million Earths could fit inside it. The Sun is about 93 million miles from Earth and provides all the heat and light that makes life possible on our planet. It produces energy through a process called nuclear fusion, where hydrogen atoms smash together to form helium, releasing enormous bursts of energy.

sections.title: The Eight Planets
sections.body: Moving outward from the Sun, the eight planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. The first four — called the inner planets — are made of rock and metal. Beyond a wide band of space rocks called the Asteroid Belt lie the outer planets. Jupiter is the largest planet and could swallow all the others combined. Saturn is famous for its spectacular rings made of ice and dust. Uranus spins on its side, and Neptune has the fastest winds in the Solar System — over 1,200 miles per hour.

sections.title: Moons, Asteroids, and Comets
sections.body: Earth has one moon, which controls our ocean tides and has been visited by astronauts. Some planets have many moons — Jupiter has more than 90! Asteroids are rocky chunks left over from the formation of the Solar System, mostly found in the Asteroid Belt between Mars and Jupiter. Comets are balls of ice and dust from the outer Solar System. As they travel close to the Sun, the heat causes them to grow glowing tails that can stretch millions of miles across the sky.

sections.title: Why It Matters
sections.body: Understanding the Solar System helps scientists figure out how planets are born, where Earth's water came from, and whether life might exist elsewhere. Space missions have sent robots to Mars, flown past Pluto, and even sent a probe into the Sun's outer atmosphere. The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, has now traveled so far it left our Solar System entirely — the farthest human-made object in history.

keyFacts: The Sun makes up 99.8% of all the mass in the Solar System.
A day on Venus lasts longer than a year on Venus.
Saturn is so light for its size it would float in a giant bathtub of water.
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth.

videos.title: The Solar System for Kids: Sun, Moon & Planets
videos.description: A colorful, clear overview of our solar system — from the Sun's size and power to the unique features of each planet.

videos.title: The Solar System in 5 Minutes
videos.description: A quick tour through all eight planets with key facts. Great for reviewing what you've learned before the quiz.

videos.title: Solar System 101 | National Geographic Kids
videos.description: National Geographic Kids explains the solar system with stunning visuals and easy-to-understand narration.

readings.title: Cosmos
readings.author: Carl Sagan
readings.citationChicago: Sagan, Carl. 1980. Cosmos. New York: Random House.
readings.summary: Carl Sagan was one of the most famous scientists who ever lived, and Cosmos is his most beloved book. In it, he takes readers on a journey through the universe — from the very small to the unimaginably large. Sagan explains how the Solar System formed from a swirling cloud of gas and dust about 4.6 billion years ago. He describes each planet with vivid detail and helps readers understand their place in the cosmos. One of Sagan's most famous ideas from this book is that we are all made of 'star stuff' — the atoms in our bodies were forged inside ancient stars that exploded long before our Sun was born. Sagan believed that exploring space was one of humanity's greatest adventures, and he wrote about it with wonder and excitement that readers of all ages can feel. Cosmos was also made into a celebrated television series. Even though it was written in 1980, its ideas about curiosity, science, and our place in the universe remain just as inspiring today.

readings.title: Our Solar System
readings.author: NASA Science
readings.citationChicago: NASA Science. 2023. "Our Solar System." NASA. https://science.nasa.gov/solar-system/.
readings.summary: NASA's Solar System Exploration website is one of the best free resources about our cosmic neighborhood. Written and maintained by NASA scientists, it contains up-to-date facts about every planet, moon, asteroid, and comet in the Solar System. The site explains how scientists study the Solar System using spacecraft, telescopes, and computer models. You can learn about current missions — like the Perseverance rover exploring Mars and the Juno spacecraft orbiting Jupiter. NASA also explains how the Solar System formed and what the future might look like as the Sun eventually grows older and larger. The articles are written in clear, friendly language and are accompanied by stunning photographs and illustrations from real space missions. Best of all, this resource is updated regularly as new discoveries are made, so it always reflects the latest science. Whether you want to know how many moons Saturn has or what the temperature is on the surface of Mercury, this is a great place to start exploring.

readings.title: A Brief History of Time
readings.author: Stephen Hawking
readings.citationChicago: Hawking, Stephen. 1988. A Brief History of Time: From the Big Bang to Black Holes. New York: Bantam Books.
readings.summary: Stephen Hawking was one of the greatest scientists of the 20th century, and A Brief History of Time is his attempt to explain the biggest ideas in physics to everyday readers — no mathematics required. In this book, Hawking explores questions like: How did the universe begin? What is a black hole? Will the universe ever end? He explains how gravity shapes everything from falling apples to the orbits of planets around the Sun. He also discusses how scientists figured out that the universe is expanding — meaning all galaxies are slowly drifting apart from each other. One exciting section describes how our Sun will eventually run out of fuel in about 5 billion years, swelling into a red giant before collapsing into a tiny white dwarf star. Hawking's writing is thoughtful and sometimes funny, and he manages to make extremely difficult ideas feel approachable. This book became one of the best-selling science books of all time because it showed that the deepest questions about the universe are ones any curious person can think about.

questions.prompt: The Sun is classified as which type of object?
questions.explanation: The Sun is a star — a massive ball of glowing gas that produces energy through nuclear fusion deep in its core.

questions.prompt: Approximately how far is Earth from the Sun?
questions.explanation: Earth orbits the Sun at an average distance of about 93 million miles. This distance is sometimes called one Astronomical Unit (AU).

questions.prompt: What is the Sun mostly made of?
questions.explanation: About 70% of the Sun is hydrogen and about 28% is helium. These are the two lightest elements in the universe.

questions.prompt: The Sun is the largest star in the universe.
questions.explanation: The Sun is actually a medium-sized star. Many stars are far larger — some are thousands of times bigger than our Sun.

questions.prompt: What process does the Sun use to produce energy?
questions.explanation: The Sun produces energy through nuclear fusion — hydrogen atoms fuse together at its core to form helium, releasing enormous amounts of heat and light.

questions.prompt: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of about 36 million miles.

questions.prompt: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface is covered in reddish iron oxide (rust), which gives it a distinctive reddish color.

questions.prompt: Which inner planet has the hottest surface temperature?
questions.explanation: Even though Mercury is closer to the Sun, Venus is hotter because its thick atmosphere traps heat in a runaway greenhouse effect, reaching temperatures of about 900°F.

questions.prompt: Mercury is the hottest planet because it is the closest to the Sun.
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest to the Sun but Venus is actually hotter. Venus has a thick atmosphere that traps heat, while Mercury has almost no atmosphere.

questions.prompt: What region of space separates the inner planets from the outer planets?
questions.explanation: The Asteroid Belt is a region between Mars and Jupiter filled with millions of rocky objects. It marks the boundary between the rocky inner planets and the giant outer planets.

questions.prompt: Jupiter is famous for a giant storm called the...
questions.explanation: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that has been raging for hundreds of years. It is so large that Earth could fit inside it!

questions.prompt: Which planet has the most visible and spectacular rings?
questions.explanation: Saturn's rings are the most visible and well-known in the Solar System. They are made of billions of chunks of ice and rock, ranging from tiny grains to chunks as large as houses.

questions.prompt: What makes Uranus unique compared to the other planets?
choices: It is the farthest from the Sun
questions.explanation: Uranus has an axial tilt of about 98 degrees, meaning it essentially rolls around the Sun on its side. This is thought to be caused by a massive collision long ago.

questions.prompt: The outer planets — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are all made mostly of gas and ice.
questions.explanation: The outer planets are often called 'gas giants' or 'ice giants.' They are made mostly of hydrogen, helium, water, methane, and ammonia rather than solid rock.

questions.prompt: What nickname is given to the outer planets because of their enormous size?
questions.explanation: The outer planets are called gas giants (or ice giants for Uranus and Neptune) because they are enormous and made mostly of gases rather than solid rock like the inner planets.

questions.prompt: Approximately how long does it take the Moon to complete one orbit around Earth?
questions.explanation: The Moon takes about 27.3 days to orbit Earth. This is called a sidereal month. The time between full moons is about 29.5 days.

questions.prompt: What causes ocean tides on Earth?
questions.explanation: The Moon's gravitational pull tugs on Earth's oceans, causing the water to bulge toward the Moon. As Earth rotates, different parts of the ocean experience high and low tides.

questions.prompt: What event occurs when the Moon passes directly between the Sun and Earth, blocking the Sun's light?
questions.explanation: A solar eclipse happens when the Moon moves between Earth and the Sun, blocking sunlight and casting a shadow on Earth. It can make the sky go dark during the day!

questions.prompt: The Moon produces its own light, which is why we can see it at night.
questions.explanation: The Moon does not produce its own light. It reflects sunlight back toward Earth. The part of the Moon facing the Sun is lit up, and we see that reflected light.

questions.prompt: In what year did humans first walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: On July 20, 1969, astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon during NASA's Apollo 11 mission.

questions.prompt: Where is the main Asteroid Belt located?
questions.explanation: The main Asteroid Belt sits between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter. It contains millions of rocky objects left over from the formation of the Solar System.

questions.prompt: What are comets mostly made of?
questions.explanation: Comets are often described as 'dirty snowballs' — they are made of ice, dust, rock, and frozen gases. When a comet nears the Sun, the ice vaporizes to form a glowing coma and tail.

questions.prompt: What forms the glowing tail of a comet as it approaches the Sun?
choices: Gas and dust pushed by solar wind
questions.correctAnswer: Gas and dust pushed by solar wind
questions.explanation: As a comet nears the Sun, solar wind and radiation push gas and dust away from the comet's nucleus, forming a tail that always points away from the Sun.

questions.prompt: A meteor and a meteorite are the same thing.
questions.explanation: A meteor is the streak of light you see in the sky (a 'shooting star'). If the rock survives falling through Earth's atmosphere and lands on the ground, it is called a meteorite.

questions.prompt: What do we call a space rock that survives falling through Earth's atmosphere and lands on the ground?
questions.explanation: A meteorite is a piece of space rock (originally called a meteoroid) that survives its fiery journey through Earth's atmosphere and lands on Earth's surface.

questions.prompt: The Milky Way is best described as...
choices: A galaxy containing our Solar System
questions.correctAnswer: A galaxy containing our Solar System
questions.explanation: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy — a massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter. Our Solar System is one tiny part of it.

questions.prompt: Approximately how many stars are estimated to be in the Milky Way?
questions.explanation: Scientists estimate the Milky Way contains between 100 billion and 400 billion stars. Our Sun is just one average star among all of them.

questions.prompt: Where is our Solar System located within the Milky Way?
choices: In the Orion Arm, about halfway out
questions.correctAnswer: In the Orion Arm, about halfway out
questions.explanation: Our Solar System sits in a smaller spiral arm called the Orion Arm (or Orion Spur), about 26,000 light-years from the center of the Milky Way.

questions.prompt: The Milky Way is the largest galaxy in the universe.
questions.explanation: The Milky Way is a large galaxy but far from the biggest. Galaxies like IC 1101 are hundreds of times larger. The universe contains billions of galaxies.

questions.prompt: What massive object do scientists believe sits at the center of the Milky Way?
questions.explanation: At the center of the Milky Way is Sagittarius A*, a supermassive black hole with a mass about 4 million times greater than our Sun. Scientists confirmed it with radio telescope observations.

questions.prompt: What force keeps the planets orbiting the Sun?
questions.explanation: Gravity is the force of attraction between objects with mass. The Sun's enormous gravity pulls on every planet, keeping them in their orbits rather than flying off into space.

questions.prompt: Which scientist described the law of universal gravitation?
questions.explanation: Isaac Newton described the law of universal gravitation in 1687. He realized that the same force that makes apples fall from trees also keeps the Moon orbiting Earth.

questions.prompt: Planets travel around the Sun in which shape of path?
questions.explanation: Johannes Kepler discovered that planets travel in ellipses — slightly oval-shaped paths — rather than perfect circles. Earth's orbit is nearly circular but still slightly elliptical.

questions.prompt: Objects in orbit around a planet are continuously falling toward that planet.
questions.explanation: Orbit is actually a continuous fall! Objects in orbit are falling toward the planet, but they also move sideways so fast that they keep missing it. This balance creates a stable orbit.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the point in a planet's orbit when it is closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Perihelion is the point in a planet's elliptical orbit when it is closest to the Sun. Earth reaches perihelion in early January. The farthest point is called aphelion.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the first artificial satellite launched into space?
questions.explanation: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957. It was about the size of a beach ball and beeped as it orbited Earth, marking the beginning of the Space Age.

questions.prompt: Who was the first human to walk on the Moon?
questions.explanation: Neil Armstrong became the first human to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during NASA's Apollo 11 mission. He famously said: 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.'

questions.prompt: Which NASA Mars rover landed in 2021 and is currently exploring the planet?
questions.explanation: NASA's Perseverance rover landed on Mars on February 18, 2021. It is searching for signs of ancient microbial life and collecting rock samples to eventually be returned to Earth.

questions.prompt: Yuri Gagarin was the first human to walk on the Moon.
questions.explanation: Yuri Gagarin was the first human to travel to space, orbiting Earth on April 12, 1961. The first person to walk on the Moon was Neil Armstrong in 1969.

questions.prompt: What famous NASA space telescope, launched in 1990, has taken thousands of stunning images of deep space?
questions.explanation: The Hubble Space Telescope has orbited Earth since 1990, capturing breathtaking images of galaxies, nebulae, and other deep-space objects, revolutionizing our understanding of the universe.

questions.prompt: Stars are born inside clouds of gas and dust called...
questions.explanation: Nebulae are giant clouds of gas and dust in space. Gravity pulls clumps of this material together until enough mass is gathered to ignite nuclear fusion, and a new star is born.

questions.prompt: What type of star is our Sun classified as?
questions.explanation: Our Sun is a yellow dwarf star, also called a G-type main-sequence star. It is an average-sized star that has been burning steadily for about 4.6 billion years.

questions.prompt: What spectacular event marks the death of a very massive star?
questions.explanation: When a very massive star runs out of fuel, its core collapses and then rebounds in a tremendous explosion called a supernova — one of the most powerful events in the universe.

questions.prompt: All stars look white when viewed through a telescope.
questions.explanation: Stars come in many colors depending on their temperature. Hot stars appear blue or white, medium-temperature stars (like our Sun) appear yellow, and cooler stars appear orange or red.

questions.prompt: What is the name for the dense remnant left behind after a massive star explodes in a supernova?
questions.correctAnswer: A neutron star or black hole
questions.explanation: After a supernova, the stellar core can collapse into either a neutron star — incredibly dense, the size of a city — or, if massive enough, a black hole with gravity so strong even light cannot escape.

questions.prompt: The 'habitable zone' around a star is the region where...
choices: Liquid water could exist on a planet's surface
questions.correctAnswer: Liquid water could exist on a planet's surface
questions.explanation: The habitable zone (sometimes called the 'Goldilocks zone') is the distance from a star where conditions are just right for liquid water to exist — which is key for life as we know it.

questions.prompt: Which planet in our Solar System do scientists think may have had ancient microbial life long ago?
questions.explanation: Scientists believe ancient Mars was warmer and wetter, with rivers and possibly oceans. Mars rovers have found evidence of ancient liquid water, making it the best candidate for past microbial life.

questions.prompt: Which moon of Jupiter may have a liquid water ocean beneath its icy surface, making it a target for life searches?
questions.explanation: Europa has a smooth icy crust and scientists believe a warm liquid water ocean may exist beneath it, kept liquid by tidal heating from Jupiter's gravity. NASA plans a mission to explore it.

questions.prompt: Scientists have already found definitive proof of life on another planet.
questions.explanation: As of 2024, no confirmed evidence of extraterrestrial life has been found. The search continues using Mars rovers, telescope observations of distant planets, and radio-wave monitoring.

questions.prompt: What molecule, essential for life on Earth, do scientists look for in a planet's atmosphere as a sign of possible life?
questions.explanation: Scientists search for biosignatures — chemicals that suggest life, like oxygen, water vapor, or methane — in the atmospheres of distant planets using powerful telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope.
